responseSet.title: Role of the 'Collated Internal Object' in Perversion-Formations
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Role of the 'Collated Internal Object' in Perversion-Formations

M. Masud Khan

M. Masud Khan

M. Masud R. Khan
3 Hans Crescent
London S. W. 1

M. Masud Khan  

It is impossible to deny that in their case, a piece of mental work has been performed which, in spite of its horrifying result, is the equivalent of an idealization of the instinct. FREUD (1905).

It is true that the piece of blanket (or whatever it is) is symbolical of some part-object, such as the breast. Nevertheless, the point of it is not its symbolic value so much as its actuality. WINNICOTT (1953).

Freud had at the very beginning of his researches established the fact that it is 'a piece of mental work' that is at the root of perversion-formations. It was to take some half-century and more before psychoanalytic theory could account for the true character and role of this mental work in terms of precocious ego development (cf. James, 1960). Starting from a very different angle, Winnicott (1945), (1960) established the necessity and role of the provided object (maternal) if the psychic fruition of the innate maturational capacities of the infant-child are to actualize. In perversion-formations, the role of these two factors is perhaps more dynamically operative than in any other style of personality integration. In this paper I shall try to elaborate one step further an argument that I have sketched out in my previous papers (Khan, 1962), (1964), (1965a), (1965b), (1968).

The hypothesis I wish to offer here is that during the infant-child stages of a pervert's development, there is a specific quality of maladaptation and/or excessive impingement by the primary (maternal) environment, which is compensated for by a precocious ego capacity of mental work that leads to the creation in the inner psychic reality of a 'collated internal object', which is the pervert's equivalent of what Winnicott (1953) describes as the transitional object in ordinary normal development. This 'collated internal object' the pervert can experience and actualize only through specific sexual events. For this argument it is necessary to establish three basic features of perversions: (1) The necessity of the presence and compliance of an external object. (2) The nature and quality of an organized phantasy-system, unconscious and unknowable, in the subject (the pervert). (3) The reality of an experiential situation in which the above-mentioned factors can be actualized. Space, motility, sight and touch are essential ingredients of this experiential situation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis and Sociology: Articulations and Applications

Robert Wallerstein and Neil Smelser

Neil Smelser

Robert S. Wallerstein
Mount Zion Hospital and Medical Center
San Francisco, California 94115
Neil J. Smelser
University of California
Berkeley, California 94720

Neil Smelser  

In marking its 50th volume with a special anniversary issue, The International Journal of Psycho-Analysis undertook to assemble an array of contributions under the general title 'Psychoanalysis Past and Present', as a way of taking historical note and assessing the present state of the field in its historical perspective. Our contribution is more prospective than retrospective; it marks an essential area of psychoanalytic (and applied psychoanalytic) advance, that our current vigour as an empirical science (of which this anniversary occasion is one of the signs) gives us reason to feel ready to essay. Our starting point is with the simple principle that human life is simultaneously both psychological and social—that both types of forces continuously interpenetrate as they impinge upon human behaviour. We shall try to develop the implications of our view that this principle is both a self-evident truth and, at the same time, a source of the most troublesome dilemmas for the student of behaviour.

A fundamental feature of organized social life is that it structures the expression of individual biological and psychological needs. This structuring has a positive and a negative aspect. On the positive side, social life provides regular opportunities for the gratification of instinctual demands. The institutions of marriage and prostitution are both contrived, in part, to permit and channel sexual gratifications. Tournaments, athletic contests, ritual rebellions (and even war) are contrived, in part, to permit the expression of aggressive impulses. On the negative side, social life structures the occasions for frustration and deprivation. Some impulses—incestuous, murderous, cannibalistic, for example—are directly prohibited by normative constraint. More indirectly, but perhaps more importantly, frustrations and deprivations often arise as a by-product of social arrangements that do not have instinctual renunciation as their explicit objective, but do lead to that consequence. Political and social stratification systems, for instance, may be established in the name of preserving peace and allocating social rewards, but they may create widespread inequity, disinheritance, and poverty, all of which frustrate and deprive large numbers of people.

responseSet.title: On the Clinical Provision of Frustrations, Recognitions, and Failures in the Analytic Situation—An Essay on Dr Michael Bálint's Researches on the Theory of Psychoanalytic Technique
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the Clinical Provision of Frustrations, Recognitions, and Failures in the Analytic Situation—An Essay on Dr Michael Bálint's Researches on the Theory of Psychoanalytic Technique

M. Masud Khan

M. Masud Khan

M. Masud R. Khan
3 Hans Crescent
London S.W.1

M. Masud Khan  

Freud, we know, had 'never been a therapeutic enthusiast' (Freud, 1933, p. 151). Throughout his life he maintained an austere reticence, verging on the pessimistic, regarding the therapeutic effects of the analytic process. In a letter to Jung (25.1.1909), we find him saying: 'To salve my conscience I often tell myself: above all, don't try to cure, just learn and earn some money. These are the most useful conscious aims' (Freud, 1961, p. 288). We are all familiar with the most modest and humble way he ends his 'Studies on Hysteria' (1895), with the following passage:

When I have promised my patients help or improvement by means of a cathartic treatment I have often been faced by this objection: 'Why, you tell me yourself that my illness is probably connected with my circumstances and the events of my life. You cannot alter these in any way. How do you propose to help me, then?' And I have been able to make this reply: 'No doubt fate would find it easier than I do to relieve you of your illness. But you will be able to convince yourself that much will be gained if we succeed in transforming your hysterical misery into common unhappiness. With a mental life that has been restored to health you will be better armed against that unhappiness.'

And in his last serious discussion of the question of technique and cure in 'Analysis Terminable and Interminable' (1937), Freud summed up his own stand as follows:

Our aim will not be to rub off every peculiarity of human character for the sake of a schematic 'normality', nor yet to demand that the person who has been 'thoroughly analysed' shall feel no passions and develop no internal conflicts. The business of the analysis is to secure the best possible psychological conditions for the functions of the ego; with that it has discharged its task.

We now know that this paper was, partially at least, a retrospective answer to Ferenczi's reproach to Freud that he had not analysed sufficiently Ferenczi's negative transference, and it is not exaggeration to say that therapeutic optimism in psychoanalysis starts with Ferenczi and his various clinical experiments and adventures. I shall be discussing the way one of Ferenczi's most enlightened and devout pupils and colleagues, Dr Michael Balint, has carried on this tradition of clinical research. From the total bulk of his extensive writings on almost every topic of psychoanalytic research I shall focus on those relating to the analytic setting and process and the role of the analyst in establishing the character, range and limits of this undertaking. It is for this reason that I have deliberately, and I hope advisedly, entitled my paper 'On the Provision of Frustrations, Recognitions, and Failures in the Analytic Situation'. It has been Dr Balint's primary and fundamental conviction that the true understanding of what happens in the analytic situation and process will derive largely from our investigations into 'the analyst's behaviour in the psychoanalytic situation, or, as I prefer to phrase it, the analyst's contribution to the creating and maintaining of the analytic situation'. It was this direction and bias of his research which made Dr Balint at first entitle his address from the chair to the Medical Section of the British Psychological Society in 1955 as 'The Doctor, His Patient and the Illness', and later in 1957 publish his work with groups of physicians under the same title. Discussing the findings of his research seminars at the Tavistock Clinic to study the psychological implications of general medical practice, Dr Balint remarked: 'In one of these seminars the first topic discussed was the drugs usually prescribed by the practitioners.

responseSet.title: Introduction to Panel on Disturbances of Male and Female Identity as Met with in Psychoanalytic Practice
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to Panel on Disturbances of Male and Female Identity as Met with in Psychoanalytic Practice

Fritz Morgenthaler

Fritz Morgenthaler

Fritz Morgenthaler
Utoquai 41
8008 Zürich (Switzerland)

Fritz Morgenthaler  

It is my intention to collect and examine psychoanalytic experience in observing the evolution of those transference reactions, the repetitive character of which indicates earlier states of sexual identity. These transference reactions could then be evaluated with a view to estimating their significance for later development of the sexual role and of sexual identity. If we take into account the cases in which there is a disturbance of sexual identity, the damage to the personality as a whole not being such that a diagnosis of psychotic development would be necessary, we are faced with a large number of clinical pictures. It is not easy to discern what they have in common.

As I have been given the task of opening the discussion and of circumscribing the topic, I intend to elucidate the problem of disturbances of sexual identity by speaking about my observations in analyses of manifest homosexuals. The clinical picture of homosexuality is pathogenetically heterogeneous. That which finally leads to a definite sexual role is grounded in manifold constellations and configurations. Psychoanalysis has adopted certain views and concepts which enable us to understand the various possibilities which can lead to a homosexual object choice.

On the basis of my own observations I wish to demonstrate how much evidence there is to show that, in the case of homosexuals, an unevenness of progression rate between developmental lines (Anna Freud) has brought about disturbances of sexual identity. I think that ego and drive development proceed at different speeds in the late preoedipal phase. Whereas magical thinking persists in the area of ego development, accelerated drive development produces lack of control in matters of sex and aggression, the result being an insufficient integration of the personality. My opinion is that, where homosexuals are concerned, such disharmony between developmental lines can be assumed because these patients experience practically every kind of sexual differentiation between man and woman in a regressive manner, in terms of superiority versus inferiority, strength versus weakness, omnipotence versus helplessness. This marked tendency proves a persistency of magical thinking focussed on maintaining an undifferentiated shape within sexual representations. Homosexual patients often come forward with stereotyped conceptions when occupied with the differences between men and women. These conceptions are undifferentiated and strike one as being foreign bodies in an otherwise articulate personality. In addition to other aspects, the importance of which is not ignored, it seems to me that homosexuality constitutes an attempt to avoid sexual differentiation and adhere to an image which is still anatomically undifferentiated, differences in power being decisive factors. The disharmony between developmental lines had as consequence the fact that the child discovered the anatomic characteristics of the sexes before a certain level of maturity in the development of the object and self representations had been reached. For that reason the child could not effect a realistic assessment of that which he had seen.

[[RunningHead]]

Computer Simulation of a Model of Neurotic Defence Processes

U. Moser

U. Moser

U. Moser
Ilka V. Zeppelin
W. Schneider
Professor U. Moser
17 Freiestrasse
8032 Zurich
Switzerland.

U. Moser  

I

The Concepts of Psychoanalysis

The great variety of information which stems from observations in the psychoanalytic situation renders formalization of psychoanalytic findings extremely difficult indeed. One first has to be satisfied with clinical concepts which, for the most part, are still descriptive in character and which make possible a certain level of communication among those who have been trained to understand them. Clinical concepts of this type are, for example, resistance, transference, instinctual desire, anxiety, etc. These first attempts at formalization have given rise to a group of concepts which come under the heading of "metapsychology". Examples of such concepts are: ego structure, superego structure, mechanisms of defence, introjects, ideals, drive, neutralization, etc. It is hardly likely that anyone would call into question the temporary nature of metapsychological concepts, and it is equally clear that a comprehensive model of the psychic processes has not been created with the concepts that have been worked out to date. Serious resistance to a deepening of metapsychological thought stems from the reductive nature of the concepts. It is rightly pointed out that the great abundance and immense variability of psychic processes cannot be given formal expression, a fact which is true even if we limit our scope to neurotic life histories. Another objection is bound up with the apprehension that theory might become ossified, leading to a loss of the intensive connection between metapsychology and therapeutic technique. With the latter criticism one can be in partial agreement when one considers how long it has taken for recent theoretical psychology to discover its technical implications (Hartmann, 1951); (Loewenstein, 1954); (Kris, 1951); (Blank, 1966). All such reproaches—others will be mentioned later—misjudge the aims and technique of formalization in scientific research.

On Formalization

All formalization is concerned with the development of concepts. It takes as its starting-point experimental data or clinical observations. The verification or falsification of concepts (the sum total of which can be called a theory) can again only take effect on the basis of empirical scrutiny.
